title: Lace cookies
subtitle: Thin, crisp caramelised oat and butter wafers
excerpt: Thin, open, caramelised biscuits that spread flat in the oven as the butter and sugar melt together, leaving a lacy, holey texture. Made from rolled oats, butter, sugar, and golden syrup with a small amount of flour to hold the structure. Fragile and delicate — best served the day they are baked.
sourceNotes: Lace cookies, also called florentine-style biscuits or oat wafers, are part of a tradition of thin, caramelised biscuits documented in American and British domestic baking literature from the mid-twentieth century. The characteristic lacy texture is produced by the high proportion of sugar and fat relative to flour, which causes the mixture to spread and caramelise in the oven, creating an open, porous structure. The recipe here synthesises the established domestic formula for oat-based lace cookies.

recipe.batchNotes: Bake in batches of 8 to 10 cookies at a time — they spread significantly and need space.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best on the day. Store in a single layer in a tin; they soften if stacked.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Watch closely in the final 2 minutes — lace cookies can go from golden to burnt very quickly. They should be amber-gold all over, with darker edges.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic open, caramelised texture of lace cookies is produced by the high ratio of sugar and fat to flour. In the oven, the butter and sugar melt and spread before the starch can set, creating a thin, porous wafer rather than a dense biscuit. Spacing is critical — place no more than 8 portions per standard tray, leaving at least 6 cm between each, because each blob spreads to three times its original diameter. Underbaking leaves them soft and chewy; overbaking by 1 minute burns the edges.

content.text: Lace cookies live and die by the

: the mixture must melt and thin out across the tray before the oat starch sets. Space the portions generously — they spread to three times their original size. The window between done and burnt is narrow; stay near the oven in the final 2 minutes.

Heat the oven to 170°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Melt the butter, sugar, and golden syrup in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and stir in the oats, flour, vanilla, and salt. The mixture will look loose.

Drop teaspoons of the mixture onto the lined trays, spacing them at least 6 cm apart — place no more than 8 per tray. Bake for 8 to 10 minutes per batch until the cookies have spread flat and turned amber-gold all over, with slightly darker edges. They will look wet and soft when they come out. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes until firm enough to lift, then transfer carefully to a rack. They crisp completely as they cool.
